nodes.label: Contact Manager
nodes.rationale: Contact type is stored separately from URL (instead of inferring type from URL) for flexibility, user customization, future-proofing, and display control. Email URLs are auto-normalized to mailto: because users naturally type plain addresses.

nodes.label: Experience Manager
nodes.rationale: Achievements are stored as a plain string[] jsonb array because stable IDs are only needed for UI editing, not persistence; IDs are regenerated from index on load.

nodes.label: Navigation Manager
nodes.rationale: Uses two-level state control: is_active (archive) plus is_visible (hide) to allow draft/staging navigation items. Section IDs are validated against a supported list at three layers (form dropdown, server validation, public UI filtering) to prevent unsupported sections from breaking the public portfolio.

nodes.label: Resume Manager
nodes.rationale: First uploaded resume auto-activates for UX; no hard delete in MVP for safety and version history. First manager with file handling, serving as foundation for future file-based managers.

nodes.label: Skills Manager
nodes.rationale: Categories are free text with no category table; order_index is scoped per category because reordering only makes sense within a category group.

nodes.label: Writeups Manager
nodes.rationale: File upload UI was deferred because the storage bucket needs manual configuration, storage policies must be added, and signed URL generation requires a service role client; Task 27 focused on the CRUD foundation.

nodes.label: Access Request Queue
nodes.rationale: Centralized admin interface so each portfolio owner reviews only their own restricted writeup access requests, with approval creating grants, rejection storing internal notes, and revocation preserving grant rows for audit.

nodes.label: Portfolio Scoping
nodes.rationale: portfolioSlug comes from the URL and is client-controlled; the server resolves slug to portfolio_id via database lookup and never trusts client-provided IDs, preventing users from injecting arbitrary portfolio_id values or manipulating records of other portfolios.

nodes.label: Role-Based Access Control
nodes.rationale: Owner/admin/editor can mutate; viewer is read-only. Enforced at three layers for defense in depth: client UI disables buttons, server actions throw for unauthorized roles, and RLS policies double-check at the database level.

nodes.label: Archive/Restore Soft Delete
nodes.rationale: No permanent delete is implemented; archiving sets is_active=false so records stay in the database, preserving data history and allowing restore. Archived records are hidden from public views by is_active filters.

nodes.label: Stable ID Pattern for Array Editing
nodes.rationale: Using editable string values (or list indexes) as React keys destroys and recreates the input component on every keystroke, losing focus. Each list item gets a stable crypto.randomUUID() id used as the React key while only the value changes, so users can type continuously. Items are cloned with fresh IDs when opening the editor to avoid stale references.
nodes.norm_label: stable id pattern for array editing

nodes.label: Icon String Key Mapping
nodes.rationale: React components cannot be stored in a database, so icons are persisted as string keys (e.g. 'mail', 'linkedin') and mapped to Lucide React components in the UI layer with a fallback icon. This lets the icon library be swapped without a database migration.

nodes.label: can_manage_portfolio RLS Function
nodes.rationale: RLS policies delegate authorization to this function, which checks for an active portfolio_members record with role owner/admin/editor matching auth.uid().

nodes.label: portfolio_members Table
nodes.rationale: Migration 003 created the table but left the membership INSERT statements commented out, so RLS denied all access until member rows were seeded manually via the Supabase SQL editor.

nodes.label: createAdminSupabaseClient
nodes.rationale: Passing the access token only as an Authorization header left auth.uid() returning NULL inside RLS policies; the fix makes the function async and calls client.auth.setSession() so RLS can identify the user. Anon key + user session was deliberately chosen over the service role key for least privilege, audit trail, and to catch permission bugs (service role bypasses all RLS).

nodes.label: Resumes Public Storage Bucket
nodes.rationale: The resumes bucket is public because resumes are meant to be publicly downloadable, avoiding signed URL complexity and latency. Paths follow {portfolioSlug}/{timestamp}-{sanitized-filename}: the slug folder enforces portfolio-scoped storage policies, the timestamp prevents collisions and gives natural version history, and filename sanitization prevents URL/path-traversal issues.

nodes.label: One Active Resume Partial Index
nodes.rationale: A partial unique index on (portfolio_id) WHERE is_active = true enforces at the database level that only one resume is active per portfolio, catching race conditions and application bugs that app-level logic alone would miss.

nodes.label: Restricted Writeup Access System
nodes.rationale: Lab writeups are kept separate from Projects because writeups contain sensitive detailed methodology needing flexible access control (public/restricted/private), while projects are always-safe public showcase cards. A writeup can optionally reference a project via project_id to link detailed content to its portfolio card.

nodes.label: writeup_access_grants Table
nodes.rationale: Stores only token_hash (SHA-256), never raw tokens, plus expiry, view limits, and revocation fields so access can be time-limited, view-limited, and revoked per grant.

nodes.label: writeup_access_logs Table
nodes.rationale: Audit trail of all access-related events (request_created, request_approved, grant_created, grant_viewed, grant_revoked, access_denied, etc.) for compliance and security auditing.

nodes.label: Server-Side Token Utilities
nodes.rationale: Token-based access was chosen over email-only access (no audit trail or revocation), shared passwords (leak), and required accounts (friction): tokens are revocable per-recipient, time/view limited, fully audit-trailed, and need no user account. Tokens are 32-byte cryptographically random values, hashed with SHA-256 before storage, verified with timing-safe comparison, and the utilities must never be exposed to client components.

nodes.label: Writeup Visibility Levels
nodes.rationale: public = direct access for safe retired content; restricted = teaser plus request/approval/token flow for most writeups; private = portfolio members only for active machines and drafts.

nodes.label: Private Storage with Signed URLs
nodes.rationale: Public storage risks URL guessing/enumeration with no access control or usage tracking. The private writeups bucket with server-generated short-lived (15-60 min) signed URLs gives revocable, logged, token-validated access to restricted files.

nodes.label: Active Machine Protection
nodes.rationale: Writeups for active (unretired) challenges must never be public to avoid spoiling live machines; active machines default to restricted or private and the system blocks the active + public combination via server validation and RPC filters.

nodes.label: Public Writeup Request Flow
nodes.rationale: violets_portfolio hardcodes portfolio slug 'violet' in all RPC calls as a single-tenant security boundary: no risk of accessing other portfolio data and simpler client code. RPC functions are required because direct table access would expose storage paths, private/active writeups, and break portfolio isolation.

nodes.label: get_requestable_lab_writeups RPC
nodes.rationale: SECURITY DEFINER function returning only safe teaser metadata for restricted, non-active writeups; explicitly excludes storage_bucket, storage_path, file_name, and file_type from its SELECT.

nodes.label: create_writeup_access_request RPC
nodes.rationale: SECURITY DEFINER RPC that resolves portfolio and writeup IDs from slugs server-side (never from client) and enforces all business rules at the database level so clients cannot bypass validation; raises exceptions with user-friendly messages that guide users without leaking internal state.

nodes.label: Storage Path Protection
nodes.rationale: Storage metadata is excluded from all public responses because direct URLs could be guessed/enumerated, bypass access control and audit logging, prevent tracking who accessed content, and cannot be revoked once known. Access must flow through request, approval, token, and time-limited signed URL.

nodes.label: Duplicate Request Prevention
nodes.rationale: Only one pending request per email + writeup combination is allowed, preventing spam/abuse, reducing admin queue noise, and setting the clear expectation that requesters wait for review.

nodes.label: Raw Token One-Time Display
nodes.rationale: Raw access tokens (256-bit, URL-safe base64) are shown exactly once in the approval success panel; only SHA-256 hashes persist, so a database compromise yields no usable tokens. Tokens are never logged and cannot be retrieved after the panel closes; the approving admin must copy and deliver the token manually until email automation exists.

nodes.label: Writeup Email Notifications
nodes.rationale: Approval-link emails are disabled by default (WRITEUP_SEND_APPROVAL_LINKS=false) until the Task 31 access page exists, so emails never contain dead links; the grant is still created and the raw token shown once in the UI. The raw token is used only transiently to construct the access URL and is never stored in notification metadata or logs. Owner notification email resolves in order: env var, then active email contact link, then skipped.

nodes.label: Resend Email Provider
nodes.rationale: The provider abstraction in src/lib/email/provider.ts isolates Resend, so swapping email providers requires updating one file without changing templates or send functions. All email env vars are server-only, never NEXT_PUBLIC_*.

nodes.label: writeup_email_notifications Table
nodes.rationale: Stores only template key, recipient, subject, status (sent/failed/skipped), provider message ID, and safe metadata; no token-containing content is ever persisted.

nodes.label: log_writeup_email_notification_public RPC
nodes.rationale: Security-definer RPC callable by anon, used exclusively by violets_portfolio server actions; it accepts only the two public template keys and derives portfolio_id from the request row to prevent forged inserts.

nodes.label: Writeups & Labs Consolidation
nodes.rationale: Violet's public portfolio no longer renders a separate Projects section; her cybersecurity lab cards move into a unified Writeups & Labs section backed by lab_writeups. The shared projects table and Projects Manager remain for Ian and other portfolios; Violet's old project rows are archived only after being copied into lab_writeups.

nodes.label: Lab Type Classification
nodes.rationale: lab_type is offensive or defensive; Violet's public filters combine lab type with machine status (All/Offensive/Defensive crossed with All/Active/Retired).

nodes.label: is_requestable Flag
nodes.rationale: Requestability is an explicit flag rather than implied by visibility: restricted labs accept requests only when true. Active labs are not automatically rejected but default to false, and admins see a warning to approve active-lab access only where platform rules permit solution sharing.

nodes.label: content_markdown Field
nodes.rationale: Stores complete public Markdown, used only for public retired or otherwise non-active labs where sharing is safe; public active full content is blocked.

nodes.label: writeup_media Table
nodes.rationale: Screenshot metadata table: public screenshots belong in the public writeup-assets bucket attached only to public non-active writeups; restricted/private evidence stays in the private writeups bucket.

nodes.label: get_public_lab_writeup RPC
nodes.rationale: Full public writeups are returned only through this RPC when the writeup is active, public, not an active machine, and has Markdown content; catalog RPCs never return Markdown or storage metadata.

nodes.label: Violet Portfolio Seed
nodes.rationale: Seed resolves Violet's portfolio_id dynamically from slug and scopes all delete/reinsert operations to it, never touching Ian's content or portfolio_members. process_steps is intentionally left empty because the reference portfolio has no process section; textual credential periods left issued_at/expires_at null rather than guessing dates.

nodes.label: Writeups Manager (Task 27)
nodes.rationale: Built as CRUD foundation first; request-access workflow, file upload UI, and public display intentionally deferred to Task 28 to keep scope focused.

nodes.label: Three-Level Visibility System
nodes.rationale: Public (full content, safe retired machines), Restricted (teaser only, requires approval, the default for most writeups), Private (not shown, for active machines and drafts) balances sharing educational content against spoiling challenges.

nodes.label: Machine Status Safety Rule
nodes.rationale: Active machines cannot have public visibility, enforced in both server validation and UI warnings, to prevent spoiling currently-available lab challenges.

nodes.label: Array Fields with Stable UUID IDs
nodes.rationale: Using editable string values as React keys causes inputs to lose focus when typing; each EditableListItem carries a stable UUID id used as the React key, separate from the editable value, so focus is preserved.
nodes.norm_label: array fields with stable uuid ids

nodes.label: Portfolio-Scoped Isolation
nodes.rationale: Never trust the client: portfolioSlug comes from route params, the server resolves it to portfolio_id via database lookup, all queries use the server-resolved id, and RLS enforces database-level isolation so cross-portfolio access is impossible.

nodes.label: Role-Based Permissions (Owner/Admin/Editor/Viewer)
nodes.rationale: Manager roles (owner/admin/editor) get full CRUD; viewer role is read-only; enforced both client-side (disabled UI) and server-side (requirePortfolioManager throws for viewers).

nodes.label: Projects vs Writeups Separation
nodes.rationale: Projects are high-level, always-public marketing/branding showcase; writeups are detailed technical educational content with flexible visibility control; writeups may optionally reference a project.

nodes.label: File Metadata-Only Upload Approach
nodes.rationale: Only metadata fields (bucket, path, name, type) are stored because the storage bucket needs manual configuration, storage policies need setup, and signed URL generation needs a service-role client; drag-and-drop upload deferred to Task 28+.

nodes.label: Private 'writeups' Storage Bucket
nodes.rationale: Bucket must be created manually as private; with no storage policies it is secure by default, and policies are deferred until file upload is implemented.

nodes.label: CONTENT_SOURCE Data Adapter Switch
nodes.rationale: Server-side env var selecting local data vs Supabase; intentionally not prefixed NEXT_PUBLIC_ because it never needs browser exposure; Supabase mode normalizes CMS rows to the same UI shape and falls back to local data on errors.

nodes.label: Portfolio Ownership (portfolios / portfolio_members)
nodes.rationale: The legacy public.admins table may remain for bootstrap/backward compatibility, but scoped admin authorization should use public.portfolio_members with owner/admin/editor roles.
